evals.assert: An untrusted-login comment carrying a byte-identical valid review marker does not make pr::has_coderails_review_for_head exit 0
evals.cmd: PATH stub gh: `api user` returns trusted login X; issues/comments REST returns one row authored by login Y (not X) with authorAssociation CONTRIBUTOR whose body is the exact review marker for pr/sha. Source git-common.sh under the stub, call pr::has_coderails_review_for_head <pr> <sha>, assert exit code == 1.
evals.negative_control: Same stub but with author login X and authorAssociation OWNER instead of Y/CONTRIBUTOR — assert exit code == 0, proving the check can distinguish trusted from untrusted and isn't vacuously 1 for all inputs.

evals.assert: An untrusted-login comment carrying a byte-identical valid eval GO marker does not make pr::has_coderails_eval_for_head exit 0, and newest-match-wins still holds among trusted comments when an untrusted forged marker is newer
evals.cmd: PATH stub gh: trusted login X. Rows in creation order: (1) trusted X posts GO marker, (2) trusted X posts NO-GO marker (newer), (3) untrusted Y posts a newer GO marker (forged). Source git-common.sh, call pr::has_coderails_eval_for_head <pr> <sha>, assert exit code == 1 and PR_EVAL_TIER reflects the NO-GO tier (i.e. the forged newer GO from Y must not win).
evals.negative_control: Remove row (3) and re-run: assert exit code == 1 unchanged (baseline trusted-only newest-wins still correct) — then swap row ordering so trusted NO-GO precedes trusted GO (both trusted) and assert exit code == 0, proving newest-wins genuinely flips outcome when the newest trusted comment changes, i.e. the harness isn't stuck returning 1 regardless of input.

evals.assert: Both readers use a paginated REST fetch (`gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{n}/comments --paginate`) and no longer call `gh pr view --json comments` anywhere in scripts/lib/git-common.sh (clean-break, no compat fallback)
evals.negative_control: Run the identical grep against the pre-change file at origin/main (git show origin/main:scripts/lib/git-common.sh | grep -c 'pr view --json comments') and confirm it is >= 1 there, proving the grep is capable of detecting the old pattern when present.

evals.assert: A matching marker posted beyond the 100th comment (i.e. reachable only via a second paginated page) is still found
evals.cmd: PATH stub gh: issues/comments REST call returns >100 rows across simulated pages (stub distinguishes by a page-tracking side channel or by returning all rows regardless of page param, since --paginate concatenates client-side); the matching trusted marker is row 101+. Assert the relevant reader exits 0.
evals.negative_control: Same stub but the matching row omitted entirely (truncate to 100 rows without the marker) — assert exit code == 1, proving the test can detect a genuine miss and isn't trivially satisfied.

evals.assert: gh failure (identity fetch or comments fetch) fails closed with exit 2 for both readers
evals.cmd: PATH stub gh: `api user` exits non-zero. Call both pr::has_coderails_review_for_head and pr::has_coderails_eval_for_head; assert both exit 2.
evals.negative_control: Same stub but `api user` succeeds and the comments REST call instead exits non-zero — assert exit 2 from this failure path too, proving both failure points are actually guarded (not just one accidentally caught by the other).

evals.assert: Full existing test suite still passes after the change (no regression to pre-existing marker/newest-wins/fail-closed/no-leak behaviour)
evals.negative_control: Introduce a deliberate one-line break (e.g. invert a return in pr::has_coderails_review_for_head) in a scratch copy and confirm run_all.sh reports a failure — proving the suite is sensitive to regressions in this code path, not just green by omission.
